responses: I currently live in Casper, WY.  I have lived here nearly 6 years.  I was born in Truckee, CA which is near Lake Tahoe.

I am a husband, father, business person, and community volunteer.  While in business, I managed various roles from account servicing, compliance, and delivering incentive payments to internal and external business partners.  In this role, I worked with auditing staff from all levels of government.  I also managed staff in each of the roles I held.

I have also been involved in various community service efforts.  I was the head of a mens group within my church, which on an annual basis put on a fundraiser for a local food bank and participated in a program to provide winter coats to children in need.   I am volunteer National Ski Patroller up at Casper Mountain. Our role is to maintain safety and provide basic first aid and safe transport of injured skiers to next level care.

The biggest challenges for Wyoming is reliance on mineral revenues which tend to fluctuate, the need to diversify economic opportunities, and spending at all levels of government.  I would start by making sure our regulatory statutes are not contributing to these challenges and we are promoting policy to encourage opportunities.  We need to closely watch spending to make sure essential services are the first to be funded without increasing taxes to support them.  And challenge government to look at different ways to provide services more efficiently and cost effective - ie. Internet Delivery, New Technologies.  I want to demonstrate to the public that government provides high level of service in the most efficient possible way.

The first effort is looking at the ability to deliver services.  I believe Telehealth is one option that can assist and we want to make sure there are no impediments in the use of this option. Another is looking into options to incentivize traveling nurses and other specialty providers that will encourage them to come provide services in Wyoming. 

Finally, we need to look at underserved areas and find ways to encourage healthcare delivery - perhaps tax incentives, targeting loan forgiveness, and promotion of rural medical training tracks.
I don't support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming.

I support the most recent legislative efforts of property tax relief as they have attempted to balance relief to the tax payer while providing funding for local revenue needs.  The legislature has offset cuts with backfill of other revenue.  I would look to make these measures more permanent which will help the state and local governments plan growth within a consistent framework.

I think the Legislature should create an economic, regulatory and educational environment that would incentivize business to develop in Wyoming and encourage existing business to strongly consider moving operations and jobs to our state.  And I think there are many opportunities to do so.  I think the current method used has not shown solid results and it appears all involved realize change in this process is necessary.

I believe in complete transparency when it comes to campaign donations.  I think the current Wyoming system works well with the exception of the timing.  I think the timing of disclosure be shown earlier than a week before in-person voting.

I live in Casper, Wyoming, where I was raised and where my family and I have chosen to build our lives.  I was born in Laramie while my father was attending school there and moved to Casper around age 5.  Other than leaving Wyoming for my education and medical training, I have spent virtually my entire life in Wyoming.

After graduating from high school in Casper, I pursued my medical education through the WWAMI program and continued with my surgical training at Dartmouth, Stanford University, and Harvard Medical School, where I served as an Instructor of Surgery.  My goal was always to bring that knowledge and experience back home.  After training ended in 2015, I returned to Casper to practice surgery and have been here ever since.

Today, I am a surgeon, small business owner, husband, father, and am deeply invested in the Casper community.  Wyoming has given me so many opportunities, and I am committed to helping ensure future generations can build successful lives here as well.

I am a Wyoming-raised surgeon, small business owner, husband, and father. After growing up in Casper, I pursued my education through the WWAMI program and completed medical training at Dartmouth, Stanford, and Harvard before returning home to practice surgery in Wyoming.

Today, I own and operate Precision Surgical, caring for patients from across Wyoming while employing Wyoming residents. Through my work, I see firsthand the challenges facing our state including healthcare access, rising costs, workforce shortages, and the pressures facing small businesses.

Most importantly, I am living many of the same experiences as the people I hope to represent. My wife and I are raising children, helping care for aging parents, planning for the future, and managing the rising costs of everyday life. I am running because I believe Wyoming needs practical, thoughtful leadership grounded in faith and service, focused on strengthening families, communities, and opportunities for future generations.

Wyoming faces challenges and opportunities. Rising costs pressure families, retirees, and small businesses, making affordability a top concern. We must maintain a strong economy, support job creators, and ensure government spends taxpayer dollars responsibly.

We also need to strengthen educational and career pathways that encourage young people to stay in Wyoming or return after training. Expanding opportunities in skilled trades, technical education, and professional careers will help build the workforce our communities need.

Affordable, accessible healthcare remains a critical priority, especially in rural areas where workforce shortages and rising costs threaten access to care.

Finally, Wyoming's public lands, natural resources, and outdoor heritage are central to our economy and way of life. We must protect these assets while ensuring they continue to benefit future generations. My focus will be practical, conservative solutions that strengthen Wyoming families and communities.

Healthcare costs and access are among the most important issues facing Wyoming families. As a practicing surgeon, I see these challenges every day. If elected, I would be the only physician serving in the Wyoming Legislature, bringing firsthand experience caring for patients and recruiting healthcare professionals to our state.

We must strengthen Wyoming's healthcare workforce by supporting the recruitment and retention of physicians, nurses, and other providers, especially in rural communities. I also support continued investment in programs such as the Rural Health Transformation Program, which helps preserve local access to care and better coordinate services across Wyoming.

To improve affordability, we should encourage greater competition and innovation in the health insurance market, increase transparency, and reduce unnecessary administrative costs. Wyoming families deserve affordable coverage and access to high-quality care close to home.

Wyoming's public lands are one of our state's greatest assets. They support hunting, fishing, recreation, grazing, energy production, tourism, and our way of life. I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer public lands out of public ownership. Once public access is lost, it is often impossible to restore.

As someone who enjoys Wyoming's great outdoors, I understand firsthand the importance of protecting access for future generations. Public lands belong to the people of Wyoming and should be managed in their long-term interests, not for short-term gain.

I support a balanced, multiple-use approach that protects public access while allowing responsible recreation, grazing, conservation, and resource development. My priority will be ensuring Wyoming's public lands continue to benefit the people who live, work, hunt, and recreate here.

Property taxes have become a major concern for many Wyoming families, especially seniors and those living on fixed incomes. No one should be forced to sell or lose a home they spent a lifetime paying for simply because property values have increased faster than their income.
I support property tax relief for homeowners while recognizing that property tax revenue is necessary to fund essential services such as law enforcement, roads, fire protection, and infrastructure. Wyoming's counties have different economies and revenue sources, therefore, a one-size-fits-all approach may not work.
As a Senator, I would work to balance taxpayer relief with funding of necessary local services. We must find opportunities to improve government efficiency, reduce spending and unnecessary burdens on property owners while preserving the services that Wyoming communities rely upon. Wyoming residents must be able to keep their homes and we must maintain strong local communities.

The Wyoming Legislature should focus on creating the conditions for economic success, not picking winners and losers. That starts with maintaining a stable tax and regulatory environment that allows small businesses to grow and create jobs.

We must also invest in the infrastructure that growing communities need, including housing, transportation, energy, and broadband. Economic growth depends on our ability to attract and retain both employers and workers.

Workforce development is equally important. Wyoming should continue expanding educational pathways, apprenticeships, technical training, and professional opportunities that help young people stay in Wyoming or return after completing their education.

Finally, we should remain open to emerging industries and new technologies while thoughtfully evaluating their long-term impacts. The goal should be sustainable growth that creates opportunity, strengthens communities, and preserves Wyoming's quality of life for future generations.

Citizens have a right to support candidates and causes they believe in. I believe voters have a right to know where campaign funding comes from. Transparency is key to public trust in the electoral process.
Wyoming requires candidates to disclose contributions and expenditures. Voters can review that information before casting their vote. I believe transparency is more important than creating restrictions that could discourage citizen participation in the political process.
If reforms are considered, they should focus on making campaign finance information easier for the public to access, while preserving the rights of individuals to support the candidates they believe will best serve Wyoming.
Ultimately, I believe elected officials should be accountable to the voters they represent. The best safeguard against undue influence is an informed electorate and a political process that allows citizens to evaluate candidates based on both their positions and their actions.
I currently reside in Casper, Wyoming. I have lived in my current home since July 2019. I have lived in Wyoming since 1978. I was born in Broken Bow, Nebraska.

I am uniquely qualified to serve Senate District 27 because of my professional experience and a strong record of community service. I work as a paralegal in family law helping families through difficult situations and gaining practical knowledge of the legal system and working with the statutes that are considered and possibly passed on the Senate floor. I am working toward my Master's in Mental Health Counseling to further help our community. I am serving in the Republican party and volunteer on nonprofit boards to engage in different facets of our communities. As a wife, mother, and Wyoming woman, I understand the values and challenges facing our communities and will bring principled, conservative leadership to Cheyenne.

Wyoming faces serious challenges in mental and physical healthcare access, the rising cost of living, and the need for a stronger, more diverse economy. 
As a legislator, I would support efforts to expand access to mental and physical healthcare services, recruit and retain healthcare providers, and remove barriers that keep care out of rural communities. I would also fight for responsible policies that will help Wyoming families such as encouraging business growth, job creation, and economic diversification so Wyoming can remain a place where families thrive and future generations can stay and build lives.

We need to recruit and retain providers to all of Wyoming. This can be accomplished by reducing unnecessary regulations that make it harder to practice in Wyoming. Provide incentives for providers to care for individuals in rural areas. Promoting price transparency, support competition and looking at possible medical liability reforms to help bring down the cost of malpractice insurance.

No, I do not support the sell or transfer of public land in Wyoming. Land management decisions should be made by the people of Wyoming. We are the owners of the land. I will support strong state and local input, protect multiple use, defend access for recreation, grazing, and responsible energy production, and making sure that our land is managed in a way that respects our heritage and way of life. Decisions should be made openly, with meaningful involvement from local communities, so that Wyoming voices are heard and our natural resources are managed responsibly.

Any solution to this must balance the homeowner and property owner with the need for counties, schools, and local services to remain funded. I support thoughtful, responsible tax relief that helps families with rising costs, while also respecting local control and recognizing the different revenue structures and needs across Wyoming. I would look for solutions that provide relief without creating unintended harm for essential services of public safety, roads, and education. Working with local governments, taxpayers, and community leaders to develop fair and flexible policies for each Wyoming community.

The Wyoming Legislature has a limited but important role in economic development. The legislature should help encourage a stable, business-friendly environment where private enterprise can grow. The legislature can help by reducing unnecessary regulation, support infrastructure, encourage workforce development, and adopt policies that help businesses invest, hire, and succeed in Wyoming.

Campaign donations should be legal, transparent, and never allowed to influence the voice of the people I am elected to serve. My campaign will be conducted with honesty, integrity, and full compliance with the law. 
Wyoming's system for monitoring campaigns should be reviewed regularly to ensure that it is transparent, easy for the public to understand, and candidates can clearly and transparently comply with campaign disclosures.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
